coredata.dc:title: Tailored hydrothermal platforms for fluorescent Q-dots with theranostic application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yijie
Han, Qun
Peng, Jianqin
Lv, Ying
Ruan, Yongming
Weng, Xuexiang
Milcovich, Gesmi
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the fluorescence properties of the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and urea under different conditions, as they have attracted considerable attention in recent years. However, so far, the detailed formation process and the precise chemical structure of the resulting fluorescent products remain inadequately understood. The results reveal that under mild conditions, fluorescence is mainly due to organic molecules, identified as citrazinic acid through acid treatment with spectroscopic techniques. Moreover, the fluorescence shifts towards carbonized quantum dots (CDs) is both temperature and reaction duration-dependent. Comparative analysis versus graphene quantum dots and citrazinic acid elucidated differences in solution properties, including excitation-dependency, photobleaching and fluorescence lifetime. With reference to previous findings in literature, this study provides for an innovative, detailed understanding on the evolution of fluorescent species, by tuning the reaction conditions. Hence, they propose for excellent future strategic application in the nanotechnology and theranostic fields.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of CDs and filtrates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra, and fluorescence emission spectra of the products under varying conditions: (A–C) different reaction temperatures, (D–F) different reaction times, and (G–I) different reactant ratios.

The emission spectra of (A) citrazinic acid standard substance and (B) solid product under different excitation wavelengths. Fluorescence decay curve of (C) citrazinic acid standard and (D) the solid product.
(A) FTIR spectra and (B) XRD patterns of citrazinic acid standard and the product. TEM images of (C) citrazinic acid standard and (D) the solid product. The insets showing the HRTEM images.
(A) 1H NMR, (B) 13C NMR and (C) high power mass spectrum of the solid product (negative ionization mode).
Proposed mechanism for the hydrothermal synthesis of citrazinic acid.
(A) XPS full spectra of CDs and N-GQDs, (B) C 1 s split peak spectra and (C) N 1 s split peak spectra.
TEM images of (A) CD1, (B) CD2, (C) CD3 and (D) N-GQDs (the inset is the HRTEM image).

Fluorescence properties of carbon dots (CDs) and dialyzed samples at different excitation wavelengths, using citrazinic acid and N-GQDs as controls. (A) The position of the fluorescence emission peak of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (B) The corresponding normalized fluorescence intensity of CDs. (C) The position of the fluorescence emission peak of the dialyzed samples at different excitation wavelengths. (D) The corresponding normalized fluorescence intensity of dialyzed samples.
High-resolution mass spectrum of the filtrates (negative ionization mode).
High-resolution mass spectra of CDs and N-GQDs (negative ionization mode).
UV absorption spectra of (A) CDs and (B) filtrate (using citrazinic and N-GQDs as reference).

Fluorescence lifetime and emission spectra of CDs and dialyzed products. Fluorescence lifetime vs. emission wavelength (dotted line) and emission spectrum under excitation at 340 nm (solid line) of (A) CDs and (B) filtrates. Fluorescence lifetime vs. emission wavelength (dotted line) and emission spectrum under excitation at 380 nm (solid line) of (C) CDs and (D) filtrates. Citrazinic acid and N-GQDs were used as references.
Photobleaching curves of N-GQDs, citrazinic acid, CDs and the corresponding filtrates.
Effect of reaction temperature on the yield of solid products.
Effect of reaction time on the yield of solid products.
Effect of reaction ratio on the yield of solid products.
Relative percentages of various chemical bonds in XPS analysis.
The optimal excitation wavelength, optimal emission wavelength and corresponding quantum yield of CDs and filtrate.

head.ce:title: Tailored hydrothermal platforms for fluorescent Q-dots with theranostic application

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the fluorescence properties of the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and urea under different conditions, as they have attracted considerable attention in recent years. However, so far, the detailed formation process and the precise chemical structure of the resulting fluorescent products remain inadequately understood. The results reveal that under mild conditions, fluorescence is mainly due to organic molecules, identified as citrazinic acid through acid treatment with spectroscopic techniques. Moreover, the fluorescence shifts towards carbonized quantum dots (CDs) is both temperature and reaction duration-dependent. Comparative analysis versus graphene quantum dots and citrazinic acid elucidated differences in solution properties, including excitation-dependency, photobleaching and fluorescence lifetime. With reference to previous findings in literature, this study provides for an innovative, detailed understanding on the evolution of fluorescent species, by tuning the reaction conditions. Hence, they propose for excellent future strategic application in the nanotechnology and theranostic fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials with promising applications in sensing, bioimaging, catalysis and energy conversion [1–3]. Their appeal stems from their low cost, facile preparation methods, and good biocompatibility [4–6]. Bottom-up synthesis approaches for CDs is often based on ready-to-go available precursors, including several organic compounds, such as carbohydrates, aromatic molecules and even plants. The available literature suggests that citric acid combined with small amino molecules are particularly effective precursors for generating CDs with high quantum yields. Interestingly, these CDs exhibit unique properties compared to traditional CDs, including ultra-high quantum yield (QY > 80 %) in the blue spectrum and excitation-independent fluorescence emission [7,8].

Studies suggest that small organic molecules might be responsible for the observed fluorescence [9,10]. For instance, Song et al. [11] identified IPCA (5-oxo-1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo [1,2-a] pyridine-7-carboxylic acid) as the primary fluorescent molecule in CDs synthesized from citric acid and ethylenediamine. Similarly, Yang et al. [12] obtained TPCA (5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo [3,2-a] pyridine-7-carboxylic acid) using citric acid and cysteine, which exhibited stable blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 83.2 %. Ehrat et al. [13] further explored the temporal evolution of CD fluorescence. Their study reported that 2-pyridone derivatives initially contribute to the blue luminescence of dots synthesized hydrothermally from citric acid and ethylenediamine within a short timeframe. However, during longer reaction times, aromatic domains within the CD structure form and grow, potentially leading to a shift in the observed fluorescence properties.

Recent studies have suggested that citrazinic acid (CzA) and its derivatives are the fluorescence sources for the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and urea. Sharma et al. used citric acid and urea as raw materials and applied a heating mantle to synthesize CDs in a N2 atmosphere. By comparing the correlation spectra of synthetic CDs and standard citrazinic acid, they suggest that citrazinic acid is the main source of fluorescence in CDs [14]. Kasprzyk et al. [15] used citric acid and urea as precursors to synthesize CDs, and analyzed the luminous composition of carbon dot filtrate after dialysis by means of liquid chromatography and high-resolution electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (HR-LC-ESI-MS). Both the molecular ion peaks obtained in positive ionization mode (m/z value 156) and negative ionization mode (m/z value 154) confirmed the presence of citrazinic acid. In addition to these findings, there are ongoing debates about the exact state of citrazinic acid in CDs solutions. Some studies suggested that citrazinic acid molecules existed freely in solutions [16–20], while others proposed that citrazinic acid molecules were bound to the surface of CDs [21–23]. Li et al. further emphasized that the luminescent properties of CzA/CD aggregates were strongly influenced by the binding processes and sites of citrazinic acid molecules within the CDs matrix [24]. While these studies have significantly contributed to understanding the molecular origin of CDs synthesized from citric acid and urea, they primarily focus on comparing the optical properties of the product with those of the standard CzA. Nevertheless, these studies have not directly isolated and identified citrazinic acid from the synthesized CDs, and as such, the precise formation conditions and the fluorescence origin in these systems remain unexplored.

Therefore, this work presents a systematic investigation of the fluorescence origins in the hydrothermal reaction between citric acid and urea, by tuning reaction temperatures, times and reactant ratios. We hypothesized that under mild hydrothermal conditions, organic molecules present in the reaction medium serve as the primary source of solution fluorescence. These species were isolated and spectroscopically characterized, using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), infrared (IR) spectroscopy, and high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) following a simple acidification process of the blue fluorescent solutions. The isolated species is identified as quinolinic acid. Extended reaction times and higher temperatures promoted the formation of increasingly carbonized fluorescent CDs, which become the dominant contributor to solution fluorescence under these harsher reaction conditions. To further elucidate the evolution of fluorescence origins, we analyze dialysis products obtained at different reaction conditions (185 °C for 2 h and 10 h, 230 °C for 10 h). By employing graphene quantum dots and citrazinic acid as reference emitters, we compared and contrasted the characteristics of the dialyzed products fluorescence originating from inside and outside the dialysis membrane under various hydrothermal regimes through excitation dependence, photobleaching behavior, and fluorescence lifetime analyses. This comprehensive approach allows for a definitive correlation between fluorescence and reaction conditions, leading to a robust set of parameters to rely on when optimizing nanoscale theranostic platforms.

Citric acid (CA) and urea were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Citrazinic acid (CzA) was purchased from TCI Co., Ltd. The aqueous solutions used in the experiment are all prepared with ultrapure water, and the rest of the reagents are of analytical grade.

The UV–visible spectra of the samples were measured with Perkin Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. The fluorescence spectrum data was collected by the RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer. FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the fluorescence lifetime of the sample. The mass spectrum of the sample was measured by the Triple TOF 4600. The X-ray diffraction pattern was determined by Bruker D8 Advance. The morphology test of the material was carried out on a JEOL-2100F TEM instrument, with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier-transform infrared(FT-IR) spectrum was measured by Nicolet 670 infrared spectrometer (4000 − 400 cm−1). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by K-Alpha XPS spectrometer (SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250), and Al Kα X-ray radiation (1486.6 eV) was used as the excitation light source. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrometer data of the samples were collected by Bruker AV 400 MHz NMR spectrometer.

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were utilized as a model to understand the carbonization process of hydrothermal citric acid and urea. In detail, graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite flakes using the improved Hummer’s method. Subsequently, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were prepared through hydrothermal treatment of GO under ammonia-rich conditions, as described elsewhere [25]. In detail, the synthesis method was set as following: 30 mL of the prepared GO aqueous dispersion (1 mg mL−1) and 0.5 mL of ammonia solution (28 wt%) were initially stirred for 10 min, then the mixture was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the filtrate was obtained by filtering with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate was dialyzed against deionized water in a 3000 Da dialysis bag for 48 h to remove excess ammonia. After the light-yellow solution in the bag dried out, the light-yellow powder of N-GQDs was obtained.

An optimized ratio (1:1–1:8) of citric acid and urea in 60 mL was dissolved in water and sonicated for 10 min. Subsequently, the mixture was placed in an autoclave within a reaction temperature range (140 − 210 °C), allowing the reaction to proceed for a timeframe set (40 min − 8h). Then, 50 % sulfuric acid was added to each solution, until the pH was about 2. Further purification was performed by centrifugal washing to obtain a solid product, which was dried out for further use.

In order to fully understand the evolution of the source of the fluorescent features of the product solution, citric acid (1.2188 g) and urea (1.3932 g) were dissolved in 60 mL of water and sonicated for 10 min to mix well. Subsequently, the mixture was placed in an autoclave, and three products were obtained under reaction conditions of 185 °C for 2 h, 185 °C for 10 h, and 230 °C for 10 h. Finally, the three products were placed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag and dialyzed for 72 h. The three types of CDs (> 1 kDa) inside the bag were labeled CD1, CD2, and CD3, and the filtrate outside the bag (< 1 kDa) was labeled F1, F2, and F3, respectively. Dry all the collected products to obtain solid products for later use.

With quinine sulfate as a reference, the QY value of the sample was calculated by measuring the integrated area of the emission spectrum of the sample under excitation and the absorbance value at the same wavelength (340 nm). The following calculation formula was used [26]:
(1)
φ x
=
φ
std
A
std
/
A x
I x
/
I
std
η x 2
/
η
std
2
φ: quantum yield, A: absorbance, I: integrated area of the emission spectrum, η: refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “std” refers to the reference standard sample, and x refers to the sample. First of all, five concentration gradient samples and quinine sulfate (φ std = 0.54) were dissolved in an aqueous solution (η x = 1.33) and 0.1 M sulfuric acid (η std = 1.33), respectively. The absorbance values of the two groups of samples at 340 nm (the absorbance value should be less than 0.01) was measure. The fluorescence emission spectra of all solutions at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm were then recorded.

Citrazinic acid, a heterocyclic compound possessing a pyridine core, features two hydroxyl substituents and a single carboxyl group within its molecular structure. The initial report documenting the formation of fluorescent citrazinic acid molecules via the reaction between citric acid and amine dates back to 1884. Behrman and Hofmann described a blue fluorescent organic molecule, subsequently named citrazinic acid [27,28]. Traditionally, the synthesis of citrazinic acid involves a multi-step process. This process requires an initial aminolysis step to generate citrate amide, followed by cyclization, deamination and hydrolysis to obtain a solid product.

To elucidate the potential role of citrazinic acid molecules as the primary contributor to the solution fluorescence observed in citric acid-urea mixtures under varying hydrothermal reaction conditions, the present study investigated the effects of acidification on hydrothermal reaction systems characterized by tuning reaction temperatures, times and reactant ratios.

As presented in Tables 1 , a remarkable blue fluorescence was observed in the reaction product solution, when the reaction temperature exceeded 140 °C, but remained below 210 °C. Additionally, acidification of these reaction solutions resulted in the formation of a solid product. At a reaction temperature of 185 °C, the yield of the solid product reached a maximum of 70.67 % relative to the initial amount of citric acid (Table 1). With the optimized temperature, the impact of reaction time was explored, while maintaining constant both reaction temperature and reactant ratio (Table 2 ). The data revealed that a reaction time of 40 min yielded to just a 7.89 % solid product. As the reaction time progressively increased, the solid yield correspondingly increased, reaching a maximum at 2 h. Further extension of the reaction time resulted in a decrease of the solid yield. Table 3 explores the influence of the reaction ratio of citric acid to urea (1:1 to 1:8) on the yield of solid products. The results indicate that the urea content plays a critical role in the formation of reaction solids. When the reaction ratio was only 1:1, no solid products were detected. Conversely, a gradual increase in the urea ratio corresponded with a gradual increase in the yield of solid products.

To investigate the spectral properties of the obtained solid product, UV absorption and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra were acquired (Fig. 1 ). The analysis revealed that the UV–vis absorption spectra of all solid products exhibited prominent absorption peaks at 230 nm and 335 nm. These peaks can be associated with the π → π* transition and n → π* transition of the carbonyl group (C=O), respectively, consistent with previous reports in literature [15,29,30]. Furthermore, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra demonstrated optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 340 nm and 440 nm, respectively (Fig. 1). The fluorescence quantum yield of the solid product was determined to be approximately 22 %.

To comprehensively evaluate the optical similarities between the solid product and citrazinic acid molecules, we investigated the fluorescence excitation dependence and the fluorescence lifetime of the solid product. Fig. 2 A and B depict the emission spectra of the standard citrazinic acid and the solid product acquired under varying excitation wavelengths. Notably, both the standard and the solid product exhibited identical spectral behavior: the emission wavelength remained invariant with respect to changes in the excitation wavelength.

In addition, Fig. 2C and D illustrate the fluorescence decay curves of the citrazinic acid standard and the solid product, respectively. Both exhibited a single exponential decay profile, with an average fluorescence lifetime of approximately 6.7 ns. These observations collectively suggest that the solid product possesses optical properties consistent with those of citrazinic acid.

To further characterize the solid product, several techniques were employed. Fig. 3 A presents the FTIR spectra of both the solid product and the citrazinic acid standard, which exhibited significant similarities. Both spectra displayed characteristic group absorptions corresponding to citrazinic acid, including ν C=O vibration at 1700 cm−1, ν C=C vibration at 1600 cm−1, ν as (COO − ) absorption at 1460 cm−1, and ν s (COO−) absorption at 1410 cm−1 [31]. Additionally, the solid product displayed other characteristic peaks associated with citrazinic acid, such as N − H vibration near 3225 cm−1, C − H vibration at 2900 cm−1, C − O vibration at 1310 cm−1, C − N vibration at 1250 cm−1, and the in-plane vibration of − OH on the carboxyl group at 960 cm−1 [32]. The XRD pattern of the solid product, as shown in Fig. 3B, revealed diffraction peaks that closely matched those of the citrazinic acid standard, with characteristic reflections observed at 8.78°, 11.75°, 13.31°, 23.28°, and 27.75°. TEM images, presented in Fig. 3C and D, further supported the identity of the solid product. Both the solid product and the citrazinic acid standard exhibited relatively uniform particle sizes, with an average diameter of approximately 2.7 nm. The high-resolution TEM images revealed identical lattice spacing of about 0.260 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite [33]. The solid-state 1H NMR (Fig. 4 A) and 13C NMR (Fig. 4B) spectra also confirmed that the solid product is citrazinic acid with high purity. Furthermore, the high-resolution mass spectrum (HRMS) of the solid product (Fig. 4C) revealed a molecular weight identical to that of citrazinic acid (m/z = 154).

Overall, these complementary characterization techniques strongly suggest that the observed fluorescence originates from free citrazinic acid molecules present in the solution under low-temperature and short-reaction-time conditions.

Based on the structures of the starting materials (citric acid and urea), a detailed mechanism for the hydrothermal synthesis of citrazinic acid is proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 5 . The reaction initiates with a nucleophilic addition, where the lone pair of electrons on a nitrogen atom in urea attacks the carbonyl carbon atom of citric acid, resulting in the formation of intermediate 2. Intermediate 2 then undergoes deprotonation, eliminating a water molecule to lead to intermediate 3. The lone pair of electrons on an oxygen atom within the eliminated water molecule then performs a nucleophilic attack on the carbonyl carbon atom of intermediate 4, resulting in the formation of intermediate 5. Intermediate 5 undergoes a series of transformations, including proton transfer, elimination of ammonia (NH3) and a carboxylic acid molecule, protonation, and intramolecular amidation. This sequence of steps leads to the formation of intermediate 10. Eventually, intermediate 10 undergoes intramolecular elimination to reach intermediate 11. This intermediate undergoes a final intramolecular rearrangement to form the more stable, π-conjugated citrazinic acid molecule.

The above experimental results confirm that fluorescent citrazinic acid is formed in the citric acid-urea system under mild hydrothermal conditions. However, at elevated temperatures and extended reaction times, citrazinic acid could no longer be isolated, despite the solution retaining strong fluorescence. To explore the origin of fluorescence under various conditions, three hydrothermal products without acid treatment were synthesized under specific conditions: 185 °C for 2 h, 185 °C for 10 h, and 230 °C for 10 h. Following hydrothermal treatment, the products were dialyzed using a 1000 Da molecular weight cutoff membrane, to isolate small organic molecules. This process led to inner fractions (designated CD1–CD3) and outer fractions (designated F1–F3). Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was used as “carbonized fluorescent dots” control to provide deeper insights into the fluorescence mechanisms.

XPS spectroscopy was employed to investigate the elemental composition of the CDs and N-GQDs (Fig. 6 A). All samples, including the N-GQDs, exhibited similar elemental profiles, with peaks corresponding to carbon (C 1 s, 284.6 eV), nitrogen (N 1 s, 400.5 eV), and oxygen (O 1 s, 532.2 eV). Table 4 summarizes the detailed quantification of relative atomic percentages for each element related to CDs and N-GQDs. A progressive increase in the C/N ratio was observed throughout the CD samples, rising from 7.2 in CD1 to 9.5 in CD3. This trend approached the C/N ratio characteristic of N-GQDs, indicating an enhanced carbonization with increasing reaction temperature and time. These findings highlight the influence of harsher hydrothermal conditions on driving the carbonization process and altering the elemental composition of the products.

High-resolution XPS analysis (Fig. 6B) revealed specific carbon bonding configurations in the CDs, including C–C/C=C, C-N, C-O, and C=N/C=O moieties. The nitrogen bonding configurations were also examined (Fig. 6C) and categorized into pyridine nitrogen (399.2 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (400.5 eV), and graphite nitrogen (401.8 eV).

Table 5 details the quantitative variations in these functional groups observed with changes in reaction temperature and time. The content of C–C/C=C bonds increased from 41 % in CD1 to 55 % in CD3, as a consequence of temperature- and time-dependent progression of carbonization. Interestingly, CD3 exhibited a higher proportion of C − C/C=C bonds compared to N-GQDs (45 %). This difference may arise from the nitrogen doping process in N-GQDs, which introduces heteroatoms into the carbon network and promotes the formation of C − N bonds over extensive graphitic C–C/C=C bonding. As a result, N-GQDs exhibited a relatively higher proportion of C − N bonds compared to CD3.

The nitrogen bonding analysis further revealed a decrease in pyridine nitrogen content from 57 % in CD1 to 38 % in CD3, along with the corresponding increase in pyrrole nitrogen and graphite nitrogen content. This trend is associated with the nitrogen bonding composition observed in N-GQDs, which exhibit a low pyridine nitrogen content (39 %) and a relatively high graphite nitrogen content (19 %). These results suggest a shift toward more stable, graphitized nitrogen configurations as reaction conditions become more intense. Moreover, citrazinic acid, as a 2-pyridone derivative, exclusively contains pyridine nitrogen. Thus, the reduction in pyridine nitrogen content in CDs, as well as the increase in pyrrole and graphite nitrogen, demonstrates a structural evolution driven by elevated reaction temperatures and prolonged reaction times. This progression indicates a transition from less stable pyridine nitrogen to more thermodynamically stable graphitized nitrogen configurations, highlighting the enhanced carbonization and graphitization processes in CD3 compared to the other samples [30,34].

The findings demonstrate the structural evolution of CDs under intensified hydrothermal conditions, characterized by enhanced carbonization and the reconfiguration of nitrogen bonding. This progression leads to the development of highly graphitized CDs, exhibiting properties that closely align with those of N-GQDs.

Fig. 7 presents TEM images of the synthesized CDs and N-GQDs, which provide for additional insight into their structural characteristics. All samples exhibited a size distribution ranging from approximately 4 to 8 nm. High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM) images further revealed a crystalline structure for all CDs and N-GQDs, characterized by a consistent inter-lattice spacing of about 0.210 nm. This value corresponds to the (102) plane of sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon [35]. These findings match the XPS results, which revealed C − C/C=C bonding in CDs and N-GQDs under hydrothermal conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and filtrates

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs, citrazinic acid, the three CDs (CD1, CD2, CD3), and their corresponding dialyzed (F1, F2, F3) were compared and analyzed under a pattern of different excitation wavelengths (300–500 nm) to encompass the excitation range of potential fluorophores, including carbon nuclei, edges and surface states. Excitation dependence is a robustly documented phenomenon in carbon-based fluorescent materials. It provides for valuable insights into the emission centers, which are often linked to the different surface states present on the CDs [36–38].

Fig. 8 A and B depicts the dependence of the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength and corresponding intensity changes on the excitation wavelength for the three CDs. As observed, both the CDs and N-GQDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission spectra, characterized by a gradual red shift in the emission peak position with increasing excitation wavelength. Conversely, citrazinic acid demonstrated excitation-independent fluorescence behavior, with a consistent emission peak at 440 nm across the entire excitation range (Fig. 8A). The optimal excitation wavelength for all CDs and N-GQDs was identified as 320 nm (Fig. 8B). This was different from citrazinic acid, whose fluorescence intensity reached a maximum at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm and was entirely quenched when the excitation wavelength exceeded 400 nm [39,40].

The excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of CDs and N-GQDs indicate their heterogeneous emissive states, which result from the presence of multiple surface defects, functional groups, and emissive traps. The similarities in the optical behavior of CDs and N-GQDs further support the role of structural evolution and functionalization in determining their fluorescence properties.

The emission behavior of the dialyzed products (F1, F2, and F3) is more complex compared to the CDs and citrazinic acid, as shown in Fig. 8C and D. F1 and F2 shared the same optimal excitation (340 nm) and emission peak (440 nm) of citrazinic acid, and their emission remained excitation-independent within the range of 300–400 nm. However, for F2, the emission peak position started to shift with excitation wavelengths over 400 nm, exhibiting a N-GQDs-like behavior. This transition suggests that F2 contains a mixture of emissive centers, including molecular fluorophores responsible for the excitation-independent behavior below 400 nm and carbonaceous structures, which contribute to the excitation-dependent emission at higher wavelengths. For F3, the emission position varied across the entire excitation range, with an optimal excitation wavelength comparable to that of N-GQDs. The evolving fluorescence characteristics of the dialyzed products from molecular-like behavior (F1 and F2) to GQD-like behavior (F3), further confirm the structural evolution of the materials under intensified hydrothermal conditions. Moreover, these results also emphasize the role of both synthesis parameters and post-synthesis processing in tailoring the optical properties of carbon-based materials.

Fig. 11 presents the UV–vis absorption spectra of the synthesized CDs and their corresponding dialyzed products, compared to N-GQDs and citrazinic acid. In CDs, the observed absorption features are typically attributed to π-π* transitions associated with the core state, n-π* transitions corresponding to the edge state and surface-related electronic transitions [43]. Conversely, the UV–vis spectrum of citrazinic acid displayed a sharp high-energy peak around 230 nm and a broader absorption band centered at 335 nm, characteristic of its molecular structure (Fig. 11A).

Interestingly, the absorption peak of the CDs exhibited a blue shift with increasing reaction temperature and time. Moreover, the absorption peak of CD3 was blue-shifted to 300 nm, associated with a decrease in intensity and a broader peak profile (Fig. 11A). This blue shift typically refers to the formation of graphitic domains within the CD structure, consistent with observations for N-GQDs [34,43,44]. These results perfectly match with the transformation processes identified under harsher hydrothermal conditions.

Fig. 11B depicts the UV–vis absorption spectra of the dialyzed products. F1 and F2 exhibited similar peak positions with citrazinic acid, displaying a broader peak at 335 nm. This observation is consistent with the HRMS analysis, which confirmed the presence of citric acid in these filtrates. Conversely, the broad absorption band at 335 nm observed for F3 was absent, indicating the effective removal of small organic molecules, such as citrazinic acid, during the dialysis process.

Fig. 12 presents the average fluorescence lifetime data for the synthesized CDs and their corresponding dialyzed products, with N-GQDs and citrazinic acid serving as controls. Measurements were performed at two excitation wavelengths (340 nm and 380 nm) across a broad emission range to capture the corresponding emission peak intensities.

Citrazinic acid exhibited excitation-independent fluorescence lifetime behavior. Its single-exponential decay profile results in a constant lifetime of approximately 6.7 ns across the entire emission wavelength range (Fig. 12A). On the other hand, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and N-GQDs displayed a similar trend under 340 nm excitation, with the longest lifetime observed at the peak emission wavelength, followed by a decrease on either side (Fig. 12A). This behavior was most remarkable for CD3, where the lifetime increased from 7 ns at the shorter emission wavelength of 380 nm to a maximum of 8.9 ns at 400 nm, before dropping back to 7 ns at longer emission wavelengths. Indeed, the emission peak of CD3 (418 nm) was blue-shifted compared to citrazinic acid (440 nm), and its broader emission spectrum matches the contribution of strong surface state emissions within the CDs.

The dialyzed products displayed fluorescence lifetime behavior similar to that of citrazinic acid, with minimal variation across the emission range and a relatively narrow spectrum, indicating the presence of molecular fluorophores [30] (Fig. 12B). To further test the low-energy surface states of CDs, all samples were re-excited at 380 nm (Fig. 12C). Under these conditions, the CDs displayed a broader emission spectrum with a characteristic red-shift associated with surface state emission, likely due to the excitation of a wider distribution of lower-energy sub-bandgaps within the surface states. Conversely, the fluorescence lifetime behavior of the dialyzed products under 380 nm excitation remained consistent with that of citrazinic acid, further reinforcing the presence of molecular fluorophores (Fig. 12D).

The photostability of CDs is linked to their diverse fluorescence origins [44]. Molecular fluorophores exhibit poor resistance to photobleaching, whereas CDs, due to their carbonized nature, possess enhanced fluorescence stability. As a result, monitoring fluorescence intensity changes in CDs under UV irradiation provides valuable insights into their distinct fluorescence emission centers. To evaluate the presence of free molecular fluorophores within the CD solutions, photobleaching experiments were conducted using citrazinic acid and N-GQDs as controls (Fig. 13 ). The relative fluorescence intensity for the three CDs and their corresponding dialyzed products were measured under continuous UV irradiation (excitation wavelength: 365 nm). Both citrazinic acid and F1/F2 exhibited a rapid decrease in fluorescence intensity during extended irradiation. After 14 h, their fluorescence intensity remained at approximately 10 % of the initial value, suggesting that a significant proportion of the organic fluorescent molecules in F1 and F2 exist in a free, unconjugated state. Viceversa, CDs and N-GQDs, owing to the protective effect of their carbonized matrix, demonstrated superior photostability [23]. After 14 h of irradiation, their intensity remained above ∼ 80 % of the initial value. Interestingly, the photobleaching behavior of F3 was observed to lie between that of F1/F2 and CDs. This intermediate photostability indicates that while F3 may contain some free molecular fluorophores, it also benefits from partial protection provided by its carbonized structure.

This study explores the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from citric acid and urea under varying hydrothermal conditions, with a focus on product composition, structure, and properties. The results reveal that by maintaining a citric acid:urea ratio above 1:2, with reaction temperatures from 140-200 °C and durations of 40 min to 6 h, blue-fluorescent CDs are obtained. Characterizations confirmed the isolated solid product as citrazinic acid under mild conditions, while extended reaction times and higher temperatures led to more complex optical behavior and increased carbonization, with a remarkable red shift in emission.

A comparative analysis showed that, as reaction conditions intensified, CDs within the dialysis bag exhibited carbon nucleus-like properties, while molecules outside the bag resembled citrazinic acid. This work introduces a simple acidification method to elucidate the fluorescence origin in CDs, revealing a clear transition from citrazinic acid to carbonized CDs with longer reaction times.

These findings offer a low-cost, scalable production platform with significant potential for future applications in nanotechnology and theranostics. The study also provides key insights into the parameters that can be tailored during hydrothermal synthesis to optimize carbon quantum dot properties for biomedical and theranostic uses.

The views and opinions expressed are those of the authors only and do not necessarily reflect those of the European Union or the European Commission.
